actions.thought: I am on the button 3-handed with Q3o, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Raising or calling here would be a negative expected value play, especially this early in the tournament. I will fold and wait for better starting hands.

AQo is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folds. I want to raise for value and to take the initiative against the big blind. A standard raise size of 3x the big blind (300 total) is appropriate here.

T5o is a very weak hand, even in the big blind. Calling a 3x raise out of position with such a disconnected and low-card hand is negative expected value. It is better to preserve the tournament stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 8h 6c. While it is a 3-handed game and widening the opening range is standard, 86-offsuit is at the very bottom of my range and generally not worth the risk of a raise against unknown players early in the tournament. Folding preserves my stack and avoids a difficult post-flop situation with a weak hand.

I am in the Small Blind position against the Big Blind after the Button folded. Suited connectors like 7c 5c have decent playability and equity. Completing the small blind allows me to see a flop with a favorable price given the deep stacks and limited field.

With Flash folding on the Button and Flashlight completing from the Small Blind, I am closing the action pre-flop in the Big Blind. My hand, 6d 7s, is an offsuit connector that is too weak to raise for value or as a bluff in this spot. Checking gives me a free look at the flop in position against the Small Blind, which is the most profitable and standard play here.

I have flopped trip fives, which is a very strong holding. Since I am out of position and have a significant advantage with trips, I should lead out for value. A bet of 100 into the 200 pot is a standard sizing to extract value from a Queen or weaker hands while protecting against potential overcards.

The board is 5h 5d Qs. I have 6d 7s, which is just 7-high with no direct draws, only a very weak backdoor straight draw. Flashlight leads out for half pot on the flop. Given I have complete air and there are very few favorable turns or rivers that could give me a strong hand, continuing here would be burning chips. Folding is the only reasonable play.

I am in the button position with Ace-Seven offsuit, which is a strong hand for three-handed play. Since I am the first to act, I should open the pot to take control and potentially win the blinds. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size in this structure to maximize fold equity and build the pot with my premium card.

KTo is a marginal hand, especially when facing a 3x raise from the Button. Calling from the Small Blind puts us out of position postflop and leaves us vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind. Furthermore, KTo suffers from reverse implied odds against the Button's raising range (which includes hands like KJ+, AT+). Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve chips early in the tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Deuce of spades. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a 3x raise to 300. While Queen-high suited hands have some value in short-handed play, Q2s is very weak due to the poor kicker and the fact that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Defending here often leads to difficult post-flop spots where I am dominated. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

QJo on the Button is a standard open-raise. We have a solid starting hand and positional advantage over our opponents. Raising to 2.5x (250) applies pressure to the blinds to steal the pot outright, and we play nicely post-flop if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 2h 4h. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x. While 2h 4h is suited, it is an extremely weak hand that realizes equity poorly out of position. Given the tournament context and the fact that I will be acting first on every subsequent street against the Button, folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the button. With Ace-Trey offsuit, I have a reasonable hand to defend my blind against a potential button steal. The pot odds are favorable enough to warrant a call to see a flop, and I prefer to keep the pot controlled rather than bloating it with a 3-bet, which might isolate me against a range that dominates my kicker.

I am out of position with Ace-high and no real draws on a King-high board. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and re-evaluate if the opponent bets, as I have little equity to justify a bet.

We flopped an open-ended straight draw along with the Queen of Hearts for a backdoor flush draw on a [Th Kh 4c] board. This board heavily favors our pre-flop raising range, giving us a strong perceived advantage. A continuation bet is a standard semi-bluff here to apply pressure, fold out better high-card combinations or underpairs, and build the pot in case we hit our straight.

I have only Ace-high on a King-high board with no significant draws. The opponent's continuation bet on this board texture represents a strong range that likely includes Kings, Tens, or pocket pairs, all of which beat my current hand. Calling out of position with minimal equity is a losing play. I will fold to avoid bleeding chips and look for a better spot.

I am on the button with 87o in a three-handed game. This hand is a standard opening candidate from this position to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early. A raise to 2.5x the big blind is a balanced sizing that allows me to represent a strong range while keeping risk manageable if I face a three-bet.

8s 5d is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially out of position from the small blind against a button open. Defending with this hand would likely lead to a negative expected value situation. Folding is the standard and correct play.
Facing a raise from the button, I hold 9-2 offsuit in the Big Blind. This is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em and completely unplayable even with pot odds. It's a clear fold.

Holding 10-3 suited on the button is a weak hand even in a three-handed game. I prefer to conserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous starting hand to play. Folding is the disciplined choice here.

With Q5o in the Small Blind, the hand is too weak to play, especially out of position against the Big Blind. The standard and safest decision is to fold here to avoid bleeding chips with marginal holdings.
I am on the button with 82 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. There is absolutely no reason to play this hand, so the correct and only logical move is to fold.
I have AdKd, which is a premium starting hand. Since the button folded, I am only up against the big blind. I will raise to 300 to build the pot for value and take the initiative in the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h-6d, which is an extremely weak hand with poor playability. Even though the price to call is 200 into a pot of 400, playing this hand out of position against a range that includes a raise from the Small Blind is negative expected value. It is best to conserve my stack and wait for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Queen-Nine suited, which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. I want to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand with position. A standard 3x raise to 300 chips is consistent with my previous play and puts pressure on the blinds.

Q7o is a weak, unsuited hand that plays poorly out of position. Defending the small blind against a button open with this range is generally negative expected value and leads to difficult post-flop scenarios. I will fold to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Flash is opening frequently from the button and is likely doing so with a wide range. A9o is a strong enough hand to defend from the Big Blind against a button open. Although we will be out of position post-flop, the pot odds dictate a call. I will defend the big blind and evaluate on the flop.

I have top pair with a middling kicker on an A-K-2 board with a flush draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking to the pre-flop raiser is standard. This board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, so checking allows Flash to continuation bet their bluffs and weaker made hands. If Flash bets, I can evaluate calling based on the sizing, but leading out here would only isolate me against their better hands.

The flop of As-2d-Kd is very favorable for my pre-flop raising range. Although I currently only have Queen-high, this is a mandatory continuation bet situation. Pro checked, and by betting approximately half the pot, I represent an Ace or a King, which should force folds from most hands that missed this board. If I am called, I can evaluate the turn, but the range advantage here is too significant to pass up a bluffing opportunity.

I have top pair with a nine kicker on an A-K-2 board. Flash, as the pre-flop aggressor on the button, will often continuation bet this board with a wide range, including weaker hands, straight draws (like QJ, JT), diamond flush draws, or total air. Folding top pair to a half-pot c-bet is far too tight. However, raising would likely only get called by hands that dominate me (like AK, AQ, AJ, AA, KK) while folding out the bluffs I want to keep in the hand. The most standard and profitable play is to call, keep his range wide, and evaluate the turn.

The 6 of hearts on the turn is a complete blank, changing very little about the texture of the board. As the out-of-position pre-flop caller, standard play dictates that I continue to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out here (donk betting) would only serve to fold out Flash's bluffs (such as missed straight or flush draws) while getting called or raised by hands that dominate my A9o, like AK, AQ, AJ, or sets. Checking allows me to control the pot size and gives Flash the opportunity to fire another barrel with air or weaker hands, against which I can evaluate a call.

The turn 6h is a complete blank that doesn't change the board texture. Having bet the flop to represent a strong Ace or King, I must continue the aggression to push Pro off marginal hands like a King, a small pocket pair, or a flush draw. Pro's check-call on the flop suggests they have some piece of the board, but they are likely capped. A check here would signal weakness and likely lose the pot at showdown since my Queen-high has almost no equity. A substantial bet of 800 (about 64% of the pot) maintains the pressure and forces Pro to have a strong Ace to continue.

Flash's double barrel on the 6 of hearts turn polarizes their range. They could have a strong value hand like AK, AQ, AJ, or a set, but they could also be continuing to bluff with a multitude of draws, such as diamond flush draws, QJ, JT, or QT. Since I hold top pair with a respectable kicker, folding now on a blank turn would be over-folding against an aggressive button opening range. My A9 serves as a solid bluff catcher, and I am getting reasonable pot odds to call. I will call the turn and evaluate the river.

The river is the 5 of spades, which is another blank that misses all the obvious draws like flush draws or straight draws (QJ, JT). I hold top pair with a nine kicker. As the out-of-position player who has check-called two streets, the standard and correct play is to check again. Leading out (donk betting) makes no sense because worse hands will fold and better hands will call or raise. By checking, I give Flash the opportunity to fire a third barrel as a bluff with his missed draws, or to check behind, allowing me to potentially win at showdown. Checking is mandatory here to keep his bluffing range wide.

I have reached the river with Queen-high and zero showdown value. My previous aggression throughout the hand (pre-flop raise, flop bet, turn bet) has established a range of very strong hands, including top-tier Aces, two pairs, or sets. Pro has check-called twice on a board featuring an Ace and a King, which strongly suggests a capped range consisting of mid-strength bluff-catchers like a King or a weak Ace. The river 5s is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture. By firing a third barrel of 1900 chips (approximately 67% of the pot), I put maximum pressure on Pro's marginal holdings. Since I cannot win this pot by checking, and Pro has shown a willingness to fold to pressure in earlier hands, this triple-barrel bluff is the most effective way to take down the pot.

The river brings the 5 of spades, a blank that misses all the obvious draws such as diamond flush draws and Broadway straight draws (QJ, JT, QT). Flash has fired a third barrel, a polarizing sizing that represents either a strong value hand (like AK, AQ, or a set) or a complete bluff with a missed draw. Given Flash's aggressive tendencies and the fact that so many draws missed, there are plenty of logical bluffs in their range. Getting roughly 3.5-to-1 pot odds (1900 to win 6650), I only need to be right about 22% of the time. My A9 serves as a solid bluff-catcher, and against a wide, aggressive button range that bricks its draws, folding top pair here would be over-folding. I will call to catch their missed draws.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong holding in a short-handed game. Opening the pot from the button is a standard aggressive play to steal the blinds or take initiative in the hand. A raise to 250 is an appropriate sizing to apply pressure.

We are 3-handed, making King-Jack offsuit a very strong starting hand. Against a Button raise, we are ahead of a significant portion of their opening range. I am choosing to 3-bet from the small blind to deny equity, put pressure on marginal hands, and take the betting initiative. This also reduces the chance of playing a multi-way pot out of position if the big blind decides to call.

I am holding 7d 5h in the big blind. There was an open from the button to 250 and a 3-bet from the small blind to 800. 7-5 offsuit is far too weak to defend against a 3-bet, especially when facing two opponents with strong ranges. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Ace-Eight offsuit is a playable hand in late position. Since I am on the button and Pro is in the small blind, I have a significant positional advantage. Pro has been aggressive in previous hands, so their 3-bet range might be wide, making a call profitable. Folding would be too tight given my position, and a 4-bet could be an overcommitment against a potential strong range. Calling allows me to see the flop in position and navigate the hand based on the board texture.

We flopped top pair with King-Jack, but the board is monotone clubs and we hold no club. This severely diminishes the relative value of our hand, as any club in our opponent's hand gives them a flush draw or a made flush, and their 3-bet calling range contains many suited hands. Out of position, checking to control the pot size and evaluate our opponent's action is the most prudent play. Betting here often isolates us against a range that crushes us while folding out weaker hands.

I have the 8 of clubs, giving me a strong flush draw on a board of Kc-7c-6c. I also hold an Ace-high which provides some showdown equity and blockers. Given the check from my opponent, I want to take the initiative and build a pot, as I have significant equity to improve to the best hand or take it down with a continuation bet.

The board is a monotone club board, and we do not hold a single club in our hand. Even though we flopped top pair, our hand is severely compromised and lacks any redraws to a flush. In a 3-bet pot, the opponent's calling range pre-flop includes many suited hands, and their half-pot bet on this flop strongly suggests a made flush, a better King (like AK or KQ), or a very strong draw featuring a high club (like the Ace or Queen of clubs). Since we are out of position and any club or pressure on later streets will make our hand impossible to defend, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play.
J7o is a weak offsuit hand with very little playability. Even from the button, attempting to steal or limp with this holding is unprofitable over the long term. I will fold.

Pro folded on the button, leaving the action between me in the Small Blind and Flashlight in the Big Blind. I am holding 6h7h, which is a strong suited connector well-suited for blind-vs-blind play. Rather than just completing the blind, I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and try to win the pot pre-flop or set up a favorable post-flop situation with a hand that has decent equity and playability.

I am in the big blind with 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the small blind, continuing with such a low-equity hand out of position is mathematically incorrect and would lead to difficult post-flop spots. I will fold and save my stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. While it's often correct to open wide from the button in a 3-handed game, my recent history shows I've been quite active, raising in hands 1, 5, 7, 8, and 10. Specifically, in Hand 8, 'Pro' called down three streets with only Queen-high, suggesting they are a 'calling station' who is unwilling to fold. Raising with trash like T4o into a player who doesn't fold is a recipe for losing chips. I will fold and wait for a better spot to exploit my image.

With Ace-Five offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, this is an excellent opportunity to take control of the hand. Raising puts pressure on the big blind, allowing me to potentially take down the pot pre-flop or maintain initiative post-flop.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, 87o is too weak of a hand to defend with from the Big Blind. While we do have position post-flop, an offsuit connector like this struggles with playability and equity realization against a raising range. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision.

I am in the Button position in a 3-handed game. I hold King-deuce suited. This is a very strong position to steal the blinds. Raising to 2.5x the big blind is a standard opening size in this spot to put pressure on the opponents and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take initiative into the flop.

QTo is a marginal offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. Even against a wide button opening range, calling in the small blind risks domination by better Qx or Tx hands, and we still have the big blind left to act who could squeeze. Folding is the most fundamentally sound and lowest-variance play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 4h. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 250. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 550), 84 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the tournament context, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand.

A2s is a decent suited Ace to open from the Button in a 3-handed game. It has good playability post-flop with nut flush draw potential and top pair outs. Raising to 2.5x (250) applies pressure on the blinds and allows us to take control of the hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 5h, a weak unsuited connector. Pro raised to 250 from the Button, which represents a wide range, but 4s 5h is still near the bottom of my range and performs poorly out of position. I have 68 BBs remaining, and there is no need to defend with such a weak hand against a Button open, especially since calling would likely lead to playing a multi-way pot out of position if the Big Blind also calls. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

The opponent opened to 2.5BB from the button. Given the pot odds of approximately 2.66:1, defending the big blind with Jack-Seven offsuit is mathematically justified as the hand has enough equity to see a flop against a wide button opening range.

I am in the big blind with Jack-high, having completely missed the flop of 6d-2d-Th. As the out-of-position player who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Attempting a bluff here would be ill-advised against an opponent's opening range that likely connects well with this board structure.

On a board of 6d 2d Th, I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. The board has diamond flush draws and straight possibilities. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands like Tx, 6x, pocket pairs, or strong draws, while folding out hands I currently beat. Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, realize my equity, and evaluate the turn, keeping the pot small with a marginal made hand.

With Jack-high on a board of 6d-2d-Th-Qs, I have no made hand or meaningful draw. The opponent checked the flop, which suggests they might be playing cautiously or hold a marginal hand. Betting here would be a pure bluff against a range that hasn't shown much strength but isn't necessarily weak. Checking is the most prudent path, allowing me to re-evaluate on the river without bloating a pot with a hand that has very little showdown value.

After checking back the flop with bottom pair, the turn brings a Queen, which hits many broadway combos in the big blind's defending range. However, my opponent has checked for a second time, which usually indicates a lack of a strong made hand, such as a missed draw or a weak pair. Betting here with bottom pair doesn't accomplish much, as better hands will likely call and worse hands will fold. By checking behind again, I maintain pot control, realize my equity, and keep the pot small as I head to the river.

I am in the big blind with Jack-high on a board of 6d-2d-Th-Qs-Ts. The opponent (Pro) checked behind on the flop and the turn. Given that it is now the river and the opponent has checked again (indicated by the 0 amount to call), there is no incentive to bet into them. My hand has no showdown value against any pair, but betting into a range that has shown caution on every street is risky. Checking is the only logical move to reach showdown, as a bluff is unlikely to force a fold from any holding that already beat my Jack-high, and I cannot win otherwise.

With a pair of Deuces and an Ace kicker on a paired board of Tens, our hand has marginal showdown value. The opponent has checked down the entire hand, meaning they likely have missed draws, King-high, or a weak pair. If we bet, worse hands (like missed flush draws) will fold, and better hands (like a 6, Queen, or any pocket pair 33+) will call. Checking behind is the most profitable play to get to showdown and realize our showdown value against their weaker ranges.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey of clubs. While suited, this is a very weak starting hand even for three-handed play. My stack is currently the shortest at the table with 68 big blinds, and I've established an aggressive image by raising frequently from late positions in previous hands. Given the strength of the hand and the fact that both Flashlight and Pro have shown willingness to play pots, there is little reason to risk chips on such a marginal hand when I can wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, 8-2 offsuit. While the pot odds to complete the small blind are decent, playing such a disconnected and weak hand out of position against the big blind is strategically poor and likely to lead to a loss of chips post-flop. I will fold to preserve my stack.

My starting hand, 3h-7c, is extremely weak and lacks any significant playability. In a three-handed game, this is a clear fold from any position, as there is no reasonable equity to justify entering the pot. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the optimal strategy.

I am dealt 6c2s in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With the Button folding, I am out of position against the Big Blind. The standard and most profitable play here is simply to fold this garbage hand.

Jack-Three offsuit is a very weak starting hand with extremely low playability and equity. Being first to act pre-flop on the Button in a 3-handed game, this hand falls well outside of any standard opening range. Folding to preserve my stack is the only sound decision.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 4d. While the hand is suited, it is structurally very weak (a three-gap hand). It has been folded to me, meaning I only have the Big Blind to beat. However, 84s is generally below the threshold for a profitable completion or raise in this position, especially since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if the Big Blind calls. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit. This is a relatively weak hand, even for a button open in a 3-handed game. My stack is the shortest at the table (68 BBs), though still healthy. I have noticed both 'Flashlight' and 'Pro' have been quite active and 'Pro' has shown a willingness to call down with potentially mediocre hands (Hand #8). While opening J5o on the button is sometimes viable in high-frequency aggression strategies, given the table dynamics and the fact that I am outstacked, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to avoid unnecessary post-flop complications.

I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind. With Jack-Nine offsuit, I have a playable hand that is too strong to fold but not strong enough to aggressively raise from the small blind position, especially out of position against an aggressive opponent like Pro. Completing the small blind to see a flop is the most standard and disciplined play to control the pot size.
Facing a small blind limp, K6o is not strong enough to aggressively isolate, but perfectly fine to check back and realize equity in position post-flop.

I am in the small blind with Jack-high and no clubs on a monotone club board (Qc-3c-8c). This is an extremely uncoordinated texture for my range, and my hand has virtually no showdown value. Betting into the Big Blind on this texture with air is a losing play as it is likely to be called by any club or Queen. The most disciplined and strategic action is to check, aiming to either fold if facing a significant bet or potentially see a free turn card if the opponent checks behind.

With King-high and no club on a monotone club board, I have very little equity. Flashlight has checked, giving us a free card. Taking a free card is the optimal play here, as betting as a bluff with zero equity is unnecessary and risky.

The turn, a seven of spades, provides no improvement to my hand, though I maintain a gutshot straight draw with my Jack-high. Since we both checked the flop, the pot remains small and the board is relatively dry despite the flush draw potential. Checking here is the optimal, low-risk approach to keep the pot small while looking to catch my straight on the river or reach a cheap showdown.

With King-high and no club on a monotone board, I still have almost no equity, and the turn card doesn't change anything for my hand. Flashlight checked again, offering another free card. There's no reason to turn this hand into a bluff right now when taking the free card is the safest and most optimal route.

The river card is an Ace, which is a card that interacts heavily with the Big Blind's calling range, making my Jack-high hand effectively worthless at showdown. Since my opponent checked, they are either trapping with a strong hand or checking to realize their showdown value with a mediocre pair. Attempting a bluff here is high-risk and unlikely to succeed, as it's difficult to represent a stronger hand than the Ace or a flush on this texture. Checking behind is the most disciplined and strategic play to minimize potential losses and reach showdown.

The river brings an Ace, and Flashlight checks for the third time. With King-high, I still have some showdown value against missed straight draws or lower high cards that Flashlight might have given up on. Turning this hand into a bluff on an Ace-high monotone board would be risking chips unnecessarily, as a bet might only get called by a better hand (a small pair, a slow-played flush, or an Ace). Checking behind to realize our showdown value is the safest and most logical play.

KJo is a strong hand in a three-handed game on the button. Raising is the preferred action to seize the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or play in position against the small or big blind. A standard open raise to 300 is appropriate given the 50/100 blind structure.

I am dealt Ten-Seven offsuit in the small blind, which is a very weak and disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the button, I have terrible equity and will be out of position post-flop if I call. Folding is the only profitable long-term play here.

I have a pocket pair of fours in the Big Blind. Flashlight raised from the Button, and Pro folded. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650) and the fact that we are 3-handed, pocket fours are strong enough to defend. I will call to see a flop, looking to hit a set or play cautiously out of position on boards that favor my range.

I have a small pocket pair (4h 4s) on a dry board with one overcard (Qd). As the caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. My hand has some showdown value but is weak, so I want to keep the pot small.

With top pair and a solid kicker on a relatively dry board, betting is the standard continuation to extract value from weaker hands like middle pairs or draws. Flash checked to me, making this an ideal spot to gain the initiative and build a pot.

On a flop of 7h-Qd-5c, I have an underpair to the board (4h 4s). Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor from the button, has a range advantage on a Queen-high board. While the 250 bet into a 650 pot is a small continuation bet, calling out of position with a weak hand that has very few ways to improve (only two 4s in the deck) is generally unprofitable. I am likely behind a Queen or a higher pair, and even if I am currently ahead of overcards, I will have a difficult time navigating the turn and river. I choose to fold and preserve my stack.
A4o is a standard open-raise from the Button in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 2.5x to try to steal the blinds, and fold if I face significant aggression.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ten-Five offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened with a standard raise to 250. This hand is significantly below the range of hands I should be defending with out of position, especially against a Button opener who likely has a wide range. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting this hand as a bluff is too high-risk given its poor equity. Therefore, the best play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

7-5 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, ranking in the bottom tier of playable cards. Even with decent pot odds of roughly 2.7:1, realizing equity with this hand is difficult, and I risk being dominated or trapped in a bad position throughout the hand. Given my stack size, it is more prudent to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation to engage the opponent.

I am on the button with 7-4 suited. While 3-handed play typically warrants a wide opening range from the button, 7-4 suited is at the very bottom of that range. Given my recent loss in a significant pot against 'Pro' (Hand #8) and my position as the shortest stack, I will play conservatively and fold this marginal hand to maintain stack integrity and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded. I hold Jack-Ten of suited, which is a strong starting hand for heads-up play against the big blind. Raising to 250, which is 2.5 times the big blind, allows me to take the initiative in the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or maintain control throughout the subsequent streets.
Facing a Small Blind open to 2.5x, King-Ten offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend from the Big Blind. I will have position post-flop, making a call a standard and profitable play.

The flop of 9s-7h-Qh provides me with a gutshot straight draw with my Jack and Ten, along with backdoor flush equity. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue to represent strength and maintain initiative. Betting half the pot (250 into 500) is a balanced approach that tests the opponent's range while building equity for potential turn cards.

The board is Q-9-7 with two hearts. We hold KTo with no hearts, giving us a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (a Jack) and an overcard. Our opponent fires a half-pot continuation bet. With only 4 clean outs to the straight, no backdoor flush draw, and the potential for reverse implied odds if a heart or a King hits, our hand is simply too weak to profitably call. Folding is the most standard and solid play to preserve our stack.

7-3 suited is a bottom-tier hand with very little equity. Opening with this hand from the button is non-standard and strategically unsound. I will fold to conserve my stack and wait for a more profitable opportunity.
